title: How To Escape 30k View Jail On YouTube Shorts

transcript.text: YouTube has a ton of ceilings, but one of the most asked questions that I've ever gotten is how to escape 30k view jail. And this is what happens when you post a video, it gets thousands of views, and then it just immediately flatlines, usually around 30 to 33k views. A lot of people think this is a content issue, and in some cases it is, but in this video today, I'm going to tell you guys every way that you can identify what is going wrong with your videos, and then how to fix it so that you can actually start getting viral breakouts with YouTube shorts and sprint some money. Okay, so like I said, a lot of people get stuck in this 30k view jail. They have no idea how to break out of it. They feel hard stuck. They feel like every single video that they post gets trapped into 30k view jail. And they aren't entirely wrong, because the amount of channels that get stuck in this range is actually just abysmal. And this is basically what happens when YouTube tests your short with a relatively large seed audience, but then doesn't push it any further. So, there are a ton of reasons why this can happen. I actually already made a video talking about this right before this one, but my audio glitched out, so we're recording this again. Drop a like and subscribe if you guys appreciate the dedication. They're all going to be important and piecing together the puzzle of why you specifically are stuck in 30k view jail and how you specifically can get out of it. Swipe rate is extremely important. I see a lot of people throwing around numbers like 81%, 80%. This is generally kind of what I aim for when I see the swipe rate. Anything below 75, I really start to panic because I don't have too many videos that have gone viral with less than 75% swipe rate. And I know this looks kind of skizzo right now, but it'll all make sense in a second. I usually aim for about 81% swipe rate. They say, "Hey Max, my video got 81% swipe rate, but it's not going viral. Why is that?" Because the number that YouTube displays for swipe rate is actually an average of the entire video. So, for example, let's say you upload a video right here and it gets a few thousand views at 90% swipe rate. Very good swipe rate. But, as it continues to get pushed, the swipe rate drops, drops, drops until eventually, right here, this point in the graph is 50% swipe rate. This is something that you have to click see more on YouTube Studio to actually see this graph. But, most people don't look at it. They just see the average and say, "Oh, I 80% swipe rate." Well, in this scenario, your video started out at 90% swipe rate and ended up at 50% swipe rate. But, on YouTube Studio, it will show you 70% swipe rate because that's the average.
